answers.field_label: Present Land Use Zoning General Plan Designation
answers.value: Existing land use: partially vacant former industrial yard with scattered storage sheds, informal vehicle parking, and disturbed grassland. Current zoning: M-1 (Light Industrial) and RD-5 (Low-Density Residential, 5 units/acre). General Plan designation: Industrial/Employment and Suburban Neighborhood Low Density. Surrounding uses include single-family residential to the north, a small commercial strip and South River Road to the east, the American River Parkway to the south, and a mix of light industrial and commercial uses to the west.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
The Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Project is a proposed infill redevelopment of approximately 48.7 acres located at the southwest corner of East Willow Street and South River Road in the City of Riverbend, Sacramento County. The project would transform a largely underutilized, partially paved former industrial yard and adjacent vacant parcels into a compact, walkable mixed-use neighborhood with a strong emphasis on housing variety, neighborhood-serving retail, and multi-modal connectivity.

Residential Component: The project proposes a total of 286 residential units distributed across a mix of housing types, including approximately 112 three-story townhomes, 94 stacked flats in three- to four-story podium buildings, and 80 units in a four-story mixed-use building with ground-floor retail. Of the 286 units, a minimum of 43 units (approximately 15 percent) would be deed-restricted as affordable to low- and moderate-income households for at least 55 years. Unit sizes would range from studios (approximately 550 square feet) to three-bedroom units (up to 1,450 square feet), with a focus on providing options for young professionals, small families, and downsizing seniors who want to stay in the Riverbend area but currently struggle to find attainable housing near services and transit.

Non-Residential Component: The project includes approximately 63,500 square feet of neighborhood-serving commercial space and 42,000 square feet of flexible office space. The commercial square footage is envisioned to accommodate a small-format grocery store (around 22,000 square feet), several ground-floor retail shops (e.g., café, bakery, pharmacy, and local restaurants), and personal services such as a barbershop, dry cleaner, and small fitness studio. The office space is planned as two- to three-story buildings oriented along the primary internal main street, targeting medical offices, professional services, and co-working space. The applicant has expressed particular interest in attracting a pediatric medical group and a community health clinic because, as a working parent herself, the project manager has experienced the difficulty of having to drive across town for routine appointments.

Community and Recreational Amenities: Central to the project is a 3.8-acre publicly accessible village green that would include a large lawn area, shade trees, seating, a small amphitheater-style berm for community events such as outdoor movie nights and neighborhood concerts, and a children’s play area with both traditional playground equipment and nature-based play features (e.g., logs, boulders, and water play). In addition, the project would construct approximately 1.7 miles of new sidewalks and pedestrian pathways, as well as separated Class II and Class III bicycle lanes that connect to existing bike facilities on South River Road and to a planned multi-use trail along Morrison Creek. A small indoor community room (approximately 2,500 square feet) would be available for homeowner association meetings, youth tutoring programs, and after-school activities. The applicant, who grew up in a similar master-planned community without many safe gathering spaces, has emphasized creating comfortable and shaded outdoor areas where parents feel safe letting their children play while they socialize with neighbors.

Transportation and Circulation: The project would construct a network of new public and private streets, including a new east-west main street that connects East Willow Street to South River Road, as well as several local residential streets and alleys. A new signalized intersection would be installed at East Willow Street and the main project entrance, with dedicated left-turn lanes, upgraded crosswalks, and pedestrian countdown signals. The internal street design emphasizes traffic calming through features such as narrow travel lanes, on-street parking, bulb-outs, raised crosswalks near parks, and street trees. The project would also provide new bus shelters and a transit stop pad along South River Road to support potential future transit service expansion. Parking would be provided through a combination of tuck-under garages for townhomes, surface lots behind commercial buildings, and one structured parking podium beneath the mixed-use building, for a total of approximately 540 parking spaces, including electric vehicle (EV) charging stations and secure bicycle parking.

Infrastructure and Utilities: The project would connect to existing municipal water, sewer, and storm drainage systems operated by the City of Riverbend and the regional utility providers. On-site improvements include new water and sewer lines, fire hydrants, and storm drain infrastructure designed to meet current standards. Stormwater would be managed through a combination of underground detention facilities and surface-level bio-retention planters integrated into streetscapes and parking lot landscaping. These facilities are designed to capture and treat runoff before discharge to the municipal storm drain system that ultimately flows to Morrison Creek and the American River. The project would comply with the City’s low impact development (LID) requirements and include drought-tolerant landscaping and high-efficiency irrigation systems. All new buildings would be constructed to meet or exceed current Title 24 energy standards and would incorporate cool roofs, high-efficiency HVAC systems, and solar-ready electrical infrastructure.

Land Use Actions: Implementation of the project requires several discretionary approvals from the City of Riverbend, including: (1) a General Plan Amendment to change the land use designation from Industrial/Employment and Suburban Neighborhood Low Density to Mixed Use Neighborhood; (2) a Rezone from M-1 (Light Industrial) and RD-5 (Low-Density Residential) to a Planned Unit Development (PUD) zoning district tailored to the proposed mixed-use program; (3) approval of a Planned Unit Development (PUD) plan establishing development standards, design guidelines, and allowed uses; (4) a Tentative Subdivision Map to create residential lots, commercial parcels, public streets, and common open space parcels; (5) Site Plan and Design Review for all buildings, landscaping, lighting, and signage; and (6) one or more Conditional Use Permits for specific uses such as the small health clinic and potential late-evening restaurant operations.

Environmental Considerations: The site has a long history of industrial and storage uses, including a former equipment yard and materials stockpiling, which has resulted in localized soil disturbance and the presence of some non-native vegetation. Previous environmental site assessments identified limited areas of petroleum staining and debris that would be remediated prior to residential construction in coordination with the appropriate regulatory agencies. The project’s environmental document evaluates a wide range of topics, including aesthetics, air quality and greenhouse gas emissions, noise, traffic and circulation, biological resources associated with nearby riparian habitat along the American River, cultural resources potentially present in deeper soil layers, hydrology and flood risk given the site’s proximity to the river, and public services and utilities. The applicant’s team has worked closely with the City’s planning and engineering staff to incorporate mitigation measures—such as construction-phase dust control, tree protection and replacement, lighting design to reduce spillover and skyglow, and transportation demand management strategies—to ensure that the project’s impacts are reduced to a less-than-significant level.

Overall Intent: The overarching intent of the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Project is to convert an underutilized, visually blighted industrial area into a vibrant, human-scaled community that provides much-needed housing and everyday services within walking and biking distance of existing neighborhoods. The applicant, who currently lives about three miles away and has to drive for nearly all errands and school drop-offs, has expressed a personal commitment to creating a place where families can realistically live with one fewer car, where older residents can age in place with easy access to groceries and medical care, and where children can safely walk or bike to nearby schools and parks. By concentrating growth in an infill location already served by infrastructure, the project aims to support the City’s climate action goals, reduce vehicle miles traveled, and foster a stronger sense of community identity in the Riverbend area.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form
